items.blurb: Once the sun drops, Lilac turns into a smoky lane of chicken intestine skewers, pork barbecue, and Adidas (chicken feet) charred over coconut shell coal. Dip in spiced vinegar, wash down with sago't gulaman from the kuya beside the grill.

items.name: Chicharon Bulaklak
items.where: Marikina Riverbanks stalls near Ecom Building, Brgy. Barangka
items.blurb: The ruffled pork mesentery here gets deep-fried until it shatters like glass, served by the brown paper bag with a small cup of sukang Iloko and siling labuyo. Locals grab it after jogging the riverbanks loop.

items.name: Arce Dairy Sorbetes
items.where: Arce Dairy outlet, Golden Valley Commercial Center, Malanday
items.blurb: Arce churns its carabao-milk ice cream right in Malanday, so flavors like queso, ube macapuno, and atis delight arrive at the freezer counter the same day. Ask for a scoop on a pandesal bun for the proper Marikeno merienda combo.

heading: What to Eat in Naga: A Bicolano Food Crawl Worth the Bus Ride
intro: Naga eats hot, creamy, and unapologetically maanghang. Pack a bottle of water, leave room in your tummy for two breakfasts, and follow this list straight off the Naga Centrum bus terminal.

items.name: Kinalas
items.where: Mang Kok's at #2 Solid St., Dayangdang; Tsa Kamot nearby
items.blurb: This is Naga's hometown noodle bowl: scraped pork or beef head meat, thick brown sauce thickened with brain, and noodles swimming in a dark broth. Dayangdang Street is basically a kinalasan strip, so taste-test two bowls in one afternoon and pick your bias.

items.name: Bicol Express
items.where: Chef Doy's on Magsaysay Avenue; Naga Garden Restaurant
items.blurb: Pork belly stewed in gata with siling labuyo and bagoong alamang, served until the coconut oil floats on top. Order extra rice before the first bite hits because the chili creeps up halfway through the plate.

items.name: Laing
items.where: Geewan along Magsaysay Avenue; Naga Garden Restaurant
items.blurb: Dried gabi leaves slow-cooked in coconut milk with ginger, garlic, and shrimp paste until they turn glossy and dark. Naga versions skew creamier than the Albay style, and the leaves should melt on your tongue, not feel papery.

items.name: Kandingga
items.where: Geewan on Magsaysay; carinderias around People's Mall
items.blurb: Chopped pork heart and liver sauteed with garlic, onions, and chili, then doused in vinegar so it sharpens up by the second spoonful. Bicolanos eat it as pulutan with cold beer, but it works just as well over hot rice for merienda.

items.name: Tilmok (Tinuktok)
items.where: Red Platter on Penafrancia Avenue; Naga People's Mall
items.blurb: Minced freshwater shrimp and young coconut, wrapped in taro leaves, then simmered in gata until smoky and fragrant. Locals call it tinuktok because of the chopping sound during prep, and you eat it leaf-and-all once it softens.

items.name: Toasted Siopao
items.where: Naga Garden on Elias Angeles St.; 3N Bakery in Centro
items.blurb: A regular asado siopao with boiled egg, but cut open and pressed on a hot griddle until the bun crisps up like ensaymada crust. Grab one on the way to Penafrancia Basilica for a walking breakfast that holds up in your bag.

items.name: Pancit Bato
items.where: Declaro's at Crossing, Divina Pastora, Bato; Bigg's Diner
items.blurb: Thick egg noodles sun-dried over firewood in the town of Bato, giving them a faint smoky tang you can taste even after stir-frying. Buy a kilo of dry noodles at the source for pasalubong, then eat the guisado version back in the city.

items.name: Pili Tart and Sili Ice Cream
items.where: J. Emmanuel Pastries, Elias Angeles; 1st Colonial, SM Naga
items.blurb: Buttery yema-based tart crowned with whole pili kernels harvested around Camarines Sur. Chase it with a scoop of siling labuyo ice cream from 1st Colonial: sweet coconut hits first, then a slow chili burn on the back of the throat.

heading: What to eat in Legazpi, Albay without burning your tongue (much)
intro: Bicolano cooking runs on coconut milk, taro leaves, and a stubborn love affair with siling labuyo. Pace yourself, order rice, and treat the spice levels like Mayon: respect the slope.

items.name: Pinangat
items.where: Let's Pinangat, National Road Poblacion, Camalig town
items.blurb: Folded packets of taro leaves stuffed with minced pork, alamang, coconut cream, and chili, then steamed until the leaves go silky. The Camalig version is the original benchmark, vendors line the National Road and sell frozen packs you can bring back to your hotel kitchenette.

items.name: Bicol Express
items.where: Small Talk Cafe on Doña Aurora St, or Balay Cena Una
items.blurb: Pork belly slow-cooked in gata with bagoong and a fistful of siling labuyo. Order it with plain rice and a soft drink because the heat builds slow, then sits on your chest like Mayon clouds.

items.name: Laing
items.where: 1st Colonial Grill on Quezon Ave, branches in SM and Ayala Legazpi
items.blurb: Dried gabi leaves simmered down in coconut milk until the sauce turns thick and oily on top. Done right it tastes nutty and smoky, not slimy, and the malls keep the spice tamer than rural carinderias if you want a softer landing.

items.name: Kinunot
items.where: Layag Restaurant near Embarcadero, or Waway's at Peñaranda Street
items.blurb: Flaked stingray or pating cooked in coconut milk with malunggay leaves, ginger, and chili. The texture is shreddy like tinapa, and Waway's old-school version still gets passed around as the cleanest take in the city.

items.name: Pancit Bato
items.where: Carinderias at Legazpi Public Market, Quezon Ave
items.blurb: Sun-dried egg noodles from the town of Bato, thicker and chewier than canton with a faint toasted aroma. Locals eat it guisado-style with shrimp and bok choy, paired with pandesal for breakfast or merienda.

items.name: Tinapa Rice
items.where: 1st Colonial Grill Express, Pacific Mall Legazpi, Landco
items.blurb: Fried rice tossed with smoked fish flakes, salted egg, tomato cubes, and slivers of green mango. It is the dish that put this restaurant on the tourist radar, and it pairs well with their pinangat and crispy pata combo plate.

items.name: Sili Ice Cream
items.where: 1st Colonial Grill on Quezon Ave (the originator)
items.blurb: Vanilla-based scoop laced with ground siling labuyo, served in escalating heat tiers from level 1 up to Volcano. The first lick is sweet, the back of your throat finds out three seconds later, bring tissues for your forehead.

items.name: Pili Nut and Mazapan de Pili
items.where: Satellite Market on Tahao Rd, plus Cagsawa Ruins stalls in Daraga
items.blurb: The Bicol pili shells out a buttery kernel sold roasted, glazed in caramel, or pressed into chewy mazapan bars. Tahao Road stalls let you taste before you commit, and the Cagsawa stretch is where you grab last-minute pasalubong before catching your Manila bus.

items.name: Tinutungang Manok
items.where: Socorro's Lakeside Grill at Sumlang Lake, Camalig
items.blurb: Chicken stewed in coconut milk that has been burned first over open flame, which gives the broth a smoky, almost roasted-cacao depth. Sumlang Lake serves it with a Mayon view that makes the lunch feel earned even when you skipped the trek.

heading: Sorsogon Eats Worth the Six-Hour Bus Ride from Manila
intro: Sorsogon cooking leans heavy on coconut milk, fresh chili, and whatever the boats hauled in that morning. Skip the SM food court and chase these into the public market, the Gubat side streets, and Matnog port stalls before you queue for the Samar ferry.

items.name: Kinunot na Pagi
items.where: Donsol bayside turo-turos; carinderias near Sorsogon City Public Market
items.blurb: Stingray meat boiled, hand-shredded into flakes, then simmered with gata, ginger, siling labuyo, and a fistful of malunggay. Donsol cooks now also swap in smoked tapas fish since shark catch is banned, so ask what is in the pot.

items.name: Kinagang (Kinigang)
items.where: Bulusan town proper kitchens; Salvan house near Gubat municipal hall
items.blurb: A Bulusan heirloom packet of lukadon (young coconut just before maturity), ulang freshwater shrimp, and hand-torn herba buena steamed inside a hagikhik leaf. You eat it with a squeeze of calamansi, and it only exists when somebody in town feels like making it.

items.name: Pinangat
items.where: Bigajo Kitchenette and House of Pinangat, Sorsogon City; public market stalls
items.blurb: Whole gabi leaves wrapped around minced fish or shrimp with chili and coconut cream, tied with abaca string, then slow-cooked until the leaves fall apart on your fork. Sorsoganons make it less soupy so the bundle holds shape when you slice it.

items.name: Baluko sa Gata
items.where: Pilar coastline carinderias; Pilar Seafoods Express by Chef Ranulfo Luzon
items.blurb: Pen shell prised off rocks in shallow tidal flats, then either fried, adobo, or drowned in coconut milk with chili. Texture sits between mussel and clam, and Pilar is the spot for it.

items.name: Cupapa
items.where: Matnog port-side eateries near the Samar ferry terminal
items.blurb: Slipper lobster harvested by hand from the rocky Matnog coast, usually grilled simple or dropped in gata so the sweet white tail meat shows off. Order it while you wait for the RoRo, since cupapa rarely travels past the port stalls.

items.name: Ginataang Santol
items.where: Magsaysay Street canteens, Sorsogon City; Gubat lunch turo-turos
items.blurb: Shredded sour santol fruit cooked down in thick coconut milk with bagoong, ginger, and labuyo until it turns creamy with a tart bite. Sorsoganons serve it as ulam over hot rice, not a side, so portion accordingly when you point at the bilao.

items.name: Alimusan Ginataan
items.where: Casiguran roadside eateries around the town center
items.blurb: Marine catfish abundant in Casiguran waters, cooked in coconut milk with ginger and chili so the firm white flesh stays intact. Each barangay does it slightly different, and you will also see the same fish grilled, kusido style, or even parmesan-crusted at newer spots.

items.name: Timitim
items.where: Salvan family house, walking distance from Gubat municipal hall
items.blurb: Grated cassava steamed with coconut milk, sugar, vanilla, and chopped pili nuts into something denser than bibingka and chewier than cassava cake. One family in Gubat still makes it every morning, so go early or accept that it sells out by midday.

items.name: Pili Tart and Mazapan
items.where: Pasalubong stalls, Sorsogon City Public Market; Bulusan pili processors
items.blurb: Buttery shortcrust shells filled with candied pili nut, plus chewy mazapan logs and crunchy pili brittle sold beside them. Bulusan supplies most of the raw nut for the whole region, so the freshness gap versus Manila pasalubong shops is real.

heading: What to Eat in Cebu City Without Wasting a Single Meal
intro: Cebu eats loud, salty, and on its feet. Pack tissues for the lechon grease, small bills for the BBQ stalls, and an empty stomach big enough to handle six rounds of puso rice in one sitting.

items.name: Cebu Lechon
items.where: Rico's Lechon Mabolo, or CnT Lechon Lahug branch
items.blurb: The roasted pig that made Cebu famous nationwide, stuffed with lemongrass and tanglad so the skin shatters without needing sawsawan. Order it chopped sa balat-balat, ask for spicy if you want the bird's-eye chili rub, and grab puso on the side to mop up the drippings.

items.name: Pork BBQ with Puso
items.where: Larsian sa Fuente, Don Jose Avila Street
items.blurb: Open-air grill alley where you walk stall to stall pointing at raw skewers (pork, isaw, chorizo, tuna panga) and they fan the coals right in front of you. Eat barehanded, dunk in suka with siling labuyo, and unwrap as many hanging rice puso as your belly can carry.

items.name: Ngohiong
items.where: Doming's Original Ngohiong on V. Rama Avenue
items.blurb: Cebu's Chinese-Filipino spring roll wrapped in lumpia paper, stuffed with ubod, singkamas, and ground pork seasoned heavy on five-spice. The vendors fry it to order so it stays loud and crackly, then hand it over with a small cup of garlicky vinegar dip.

items.name: Tuslob Buwa
items.where: Azul Tuslob Buwa Mabolo, or Pasil Fish Port alley stalls
items.blurb: Communal pot bubbling with sauteed pig brain and liver simmered in garlic, soy, and shrimp paste until it froths over. You squat on plastic stools, dunk puso straight into the buwa (foam), and pass the dipping stick around with strangers.

items.name: Sutukil Seafood
items.where: STK ta Bay! on Orchid Street, Capitol Site
items.blurb: You pick live grouper, prawns, or squid from the ice trays and choose three ways at once: sugba (grilled over coals), tola (clear ginger broth), and kilaw (raw, soaked in vinegar with ginger and red onion). It is the fastest way to taste Mactan's catch without going to a resort.

items.name: Humba
items.where: Carbon Market carinderias on M.C. Briones Street
items.blurb: Slow-braised pork belly stew darkened with soy, vinegar, brown sugar, and fermented tausi black beans, often loaded with dried banana blossoms. Locals order it on a Sunday lunch over hot rice, and the fat just slides off the bone.

items.name: Chorizo de Cebu
items.where: Taboan Public Market dry goods section
items.blurb: Short, plump cured sausage tied in pairs with abaca twine, sharper and garlickier than the sweet Pampanga style. Vendors will fry a sample for you, but most travelers buy a kilo vacuum-packed to take home for silog breakfasts.

items.name: Dried Mango
items.where: 7D factory outlet in Tipolo Mandaue
items.blurb: Carabao mangoes sliced thin and slow-dried until they go chewy and sticky, with that sour-sweet snap that no other province quite copies. Taboan stalls sell them by the kilo loose so you can taste before you commit, which is cheaper than the airport shelves.

items.name: Mandaue Bibingka
items.where: Tres Marias Bibingkahan, near Mandaue City Hall
items.blurb: Fermented rice cake baked inurno style with charcoal piled both under and over a clay pot, giving it a smoky brown crust and a soft pull-apart center. Locals eat it warm with sikwate (thick native chocolate) for breakfast, and the morning batch usually sells out before 8am.

heading: Iloilo City Food Trip: 8 Dishes You Better Not Skip, Ilonggo Style
intro: Iloilo isn't just UNESCO's first PH City of Gastronomy for the title, it actually backs it up bowl by bowl. Come hungry, wear pants with give, and pace yourself across La Paz, Molo, Jaro, and Arevalo.

items.name: La Paz Batchoy
items.where: Deco's, Ted's Oldtimer, Netong's inside La Paz Public Market
items.blurb: The OG noodle soup of Iloilo, traced to 1930s La Paz Market butcher Federico 'Deco' Guillergan Sr. Miki, pork liver and kidney, beef loin, raw egg dropped in hot broth, heavy shower of chicharon. Eat it at the OG market stalls.

items.name: Pancit Molo
items.where: Kap Ising's in South San Jose, Molo and Panaderia de Molo near Molo Church
items.blurb: Born in the old Chinese district of Molo, this is the Ilonggo answer to wonton soup: pork-shrimp dumplings in clear chicken broth with pulled chicken, fried garlic, and spring onions. Kap Ising's is the locals' pick across town from the coral-stone church.

items.name: KBL (Kadyos, Baboy, Langka)
items.where: Tatoy's Manokan in Arevalo and Stella in Greenfield Complex along Diversion Road
items.blurb: Sour pork stew built on three Panay staples: kadyos (purple pigeon peas only grown around here), baboy pata, and unripe langka. The souring agent is batwan, a tamarind cousin endemic to Panay forests, so the tang hits different from sinigang sa sampaloc.

items.name: Kansi
items.where: Pat-Pat's Kansi on Diversion Road, branch open since 1999
items.blurb: Imagine bulalo and sinigang had a baby in Western Visayas, then add batwan. Beef shank with fat marrow simmers with unripe jackfruit and lemongrass until the bone slides clean, served shimmering orange from atsuete. Pat-Pat's is where Ilonggos send first-timers.

items.name: Tinuom na Manok
items.where: Iloilo Central Market food stalls and homestyle carinderias around Jaro plaza
items.blurb: Native darag chicken bundled in banana leaves with tanglad, onions, and tomatoes, then steamed in its own juices until the broth runs herbal and clean. Quieter than inasal, but Ilonggos treat it as the truer test of a kitchen since there's nowhere to hide.

items.name: Native Lechon Manok (Darag)
items.where: Tatoy's Manokan & Seafoods, Sto. Nino Sur, Arevalo (Villa Beach strip)
items.blurb: Bisaya nga manok rubbed with vinegar and calamansi, stuffed with tamarind leaves and tanglad, then turned slowly over charcoal until the skin shatters. Darag is a Western Visayas native strain with darker, gamier meat that eats nothing like the Jollibee bird.

items.name: Talaba at Diwal sa Villa Beach
items.where: Breakthrough Restaurant, Brgy. Sto. Nino Norte, Arevalo, Villa Beach
items.blurb: Arevalo's coastline is talaba country, and Breakthrough plates the creamy oysters fresh from the bay with seasonal diwal (angel wing clams) grilled in butter and garlic. Sit on the seaside extension, order baked talaba and managat, and ask for extra suka.

items.name: Queen Siopao at Biscocho
items.where: Roberto's on JM Basa, Calle Real; Biscocho Haus, Lopez Jaena, Jaro
items.blurb: Roberto's seven-inch Queen is stuffed with Chinese sausage, adobo, bacon, and salted egg, sold only in Calle Real's old downtown. Then run to Biscocho Haus in Jaro for twice-baked biscocho, barquillos, and that chewy butterscotch your titas will ask for.

heading: What to Eat in Bacolod Before the Plane Even Lands
intro: City of Smiles, but really City of Smoke since every other corner is grilling chicken over coconut husk coals. Come hungry, bring tissue for the chicken oil, and pace yourself because pasalubong shopping at the airport counts as a final boss meal.

items.name: Chicken Inasal (Pecho or Paa)
items.where: Manokan Country beside SM City Bacolod, Aida's or Nena's Rose
items.blurb: Order the paa cut, dunk it in the chicken oil and sinamak vinegar with siling labuyo, then pour everything over rice. The marinade is calamansi, coconut vinegar, tanglad, and atsuete which is why the skin glows orange and tastes a little smoky from the husk fire.

items.name: Kansi
items.where: Sharyn's Cansi House at Capitol Shopping Center, or Eron's on Narra Avenue
items.blurb: Beef shank simmered until the bone marrow slides out, soured with batuan fruit instead of sampalok. Sharyn's also serves a Fried Kansi that made it onto the World Street Food Congress shortlist, so try both wet and crispy versions if you can stomach it.

items.name: KBL (Kadyos, Baboy, Langka)
items.where: Sandok at Robinsons Place Bacolod, or Imay's in the city proper
items.blurb: Black pigeon peas, pata cuts, and unripe nangka stewed with batuan for that signature sour kick. The broth turns a muddy purple from the kadyos and tastes like Negrense home cooking that you cannot really get outside the Visayas.

items.name: Pancit Molo
items.where: Bulldog Inn in Mandalagan, or Kyle's Eatery for a smaller bowl
items.blurb: Wonton-style pork dumplings floating in a clear chicken broth, topped with toasted garlic and spring onion. Bulldog Inn has been doing it the old way for decades and the wrappers stay silky instead of turning to mush.

items.name: Batchoy with Lechon (Batchon)
items.where: Pendy's Snack Bar along Lacson Street
items.blurb: Pendy's claims this is the original La Paz batchoy formula from the Maravilla family, topped with crackly lechon kawali instead of just chicharon bits. Pair it with their Half Moon pastry for merienda since both are house signatures.

items.name: Piaya
items.where: BongBong's main store on Alijis Road, or any Bacolod-Silay Airport stall
items.blurb: Flat unleavened bread stuffed with muscovado from the Negros sugar mills, toasted till the filling oozes. Get the ube and mango variants from BongBong's, and grab barquillos in the same run since they sit on the same shelf.

items.name: Napoleones
items.where: Merzci Pasalubong on Lacson Street, or Bon's near the Capitol
items.blurb: Layered puff pastry stuffed with custard cream and capped with a crackly white glaze. Merzci is the volume player but the Bon version has a softer pastry that locals argue about endlessly. Eat them the same day since the glaze sweats in the heat.

items.name: Inasal Innards (Isol, Atay, Baticolon)
items.where: Manokan Country stalls, ask for the skewer tray at Aida's
items.blurb: Skip the pecho once and try the chicken butt (isol), liver (atay), and gizzard (baticolon) skewers basted with the same atsuete oil. The isol is rendered crispy on the outside and stays jiggly inside, which is the whole point if you grew up eating street tusok-tusok.

items.name: Butterscotch Bars
items.where: BongBong's Pasalubong Center, or Merzci near Lopue's East
items.blurb: Chewy muscovado-and-cashew squares wrapped individually in waxed paper, made dense enough to survive a tricycle ride home. Buy a box for the office and one for the plane, you will finish the plane box before takeoff.

heading: Dumaguete Eats: The Sweet, Smoky, Sea-Salty Plates of Negros Oriental
intro: Dumaguete is the kind of place where breakfast comes wrapped in banana leaves and dessert is older than your lola. Here is what to chew through between Rizal Boulevard sunsets and tricycle rides to Apo Island.

items.name: Silvanas
items.where: Sans Rival Cakes and Pastries, San Jose Street near Rizal Boulevard
items.blurb: Two cashew-meringue wafers hugging a buttercream filling, rolled in cookie crumbs. Trining Sagarbarria flattened the original mound shape into the disc Dumagueteños now ship home by the dozen. Grab a frozen pasalubong box for the bus ride.

items.name: Sans Rival
items.where: Sans Rival main branch, San Jose Street
items.blurb: Layered cashew dacquoise stacked with French buttercream, sliced cold from the chiller. The Sagarbarria family has been baking it since 1977, and locals will argue this is the gold standard for the country. Order by the slice.

items.name: Budbud Kabog
items.where: Painitan stalls, Building 3, Dumaguete Public Market, Perdices Street
items.blurb: Suman made from millet seed instead of glutinous rice, hauled in from Tanjay where lolas wake at 3am to wrap it. Steamed in banana leaves with coconut, eaten with sugar-dusted ripe mango on the side. Hit the Bishop Surban Street side.

items.name: Puto Maya with Sikwate
items.where: Painitan, Dumaguete Public Market Building 3, before 8am
items.blurb: Sticky rice steamed in coconut milk and ginger, served with a clay mug of tablea hot chocolate. Ask for the 10-5-5-5 combo and you get puto, sikwate, peanut butter, and condensed milk. The cacao is grainy in the best way.

items.name: Chicken Inato
items.where: Jo's Chicken Inato, Silliman Avenue original branch
items.blurb: Charcoal-grilled chicken brushed with a sweeter, less salty marinade than your usual Bacolod inasal. Jesse and Josephine Ng started it in 1985 and the name comes from atin ito, meaning ours. Pair the petso with garlic rice and atchara.

items.name: Boulevard Tempura and Squid Balls
items.where: Tempurahan stalls, Rizal Boulevard seawall, from 5pm
items.blurb: Skewered fish paste sticks and squid balls fried in bubbling oil under the acacia trees, dunked in sweet or spicy sauce. The strip lights up after sundown when joggers, students, and tambay families take over the benches. Bring coins, vendors hate big bills.

items.name: Kinilaw na Tanigue
items.where: Lab-as Seafood Restaurant, Flores Avenue
items.blurb: Cubes of raw spanish mackerel cured in coconut vinegar, calamansi, ginger, siling labuyo, and red onion. The fish is bought that morning straight off Visayan Sea bancas, so the flesh stays firm. Order sutukil-style at the palengke too.

items.name: Budbud Pilit with Tsokolate
items.where: Painitan stalls, Dumaguete Public Market
items.blurb: The glutinous-rice cousin to budbud kabog, wrapped tight in banana leaves and steamed until each grain glues to the next. Locals dip it into the same sikwate cup, sometimes with a slab of salty kesong puti from Bais on the side.

heading: What to Eat in Boracay, Aklan: The Pinoy DIY Food Run
intro: Boracay is not just sunset cocktails on White Beach. The Aklan side of the menu hides banana-stalk stews, bamboo-cooked native chicken, and a sausage Kalibo will fight you over. Bring small bills and a real appetite.

items.name: Inubarang Manok
items.where: Aklanon eateries in Kalibo and Malay; ask resort cooks to prep it
items.blurb: Native chicken simmered in coconut milk with shredded ubad, the soft pith of the banana trunk, plus lemongrass and libas leaves for a gentle sour kick. Tastes like an Aklanon lola made it. Order it whenever you see it chalked up.

items.name: Binakoe (Bamboo Chicken)
items.where: Festival stalls in Kalibo and Ati-Atihan food tents; some Boracay native restos
items.blurb: Native chicken stuffed inside a fresh bamboo node with onions, lemongrass, and aeabihig leaves, then roasted over fire so the bamboo sap perfumes the broth. Drinks like sinigang but smokier. Slurp it straight from the bamboo.

items.name: Chorizo de Kalibo
items.where: Kalibo public market stalls; pasalubong shops along Roxas Avenue, Kalibo
items.blurb: Kalibo's own longganisa is a tight little sausage of ground pork, garlic, and house spice mix that lands sweet, salty, and porky all at once. Locals fry it for almusal with sinangag and a runny egg. Buy a frozen kilo to take home.

items.name: Paluto Seafood
items.where: D'Talipapa wet market, Station 2, just behind D'Mall
items.blurb: Walk the stalls, haggle for live prawns, lapu-lapu, scallops, or curacha, then hand the bag to a paluto kitchen across the aisle. Pay per kilo for grilled, steamed, or buttered-garlic prep. Cheapest seafood on the island if you bargain.

items.name: Taeaba (Aklan Oysters)
items.where: D'Talipapa paluto counters; brought in fresh from New Washington, Aklan
items.blurb: Mangrove oysters from New Washington come briny with a clean sweet finish, nothing like the muddy ones up north. Order them raw with sukang Iloko, siling labuyo, and a calamansi squeeze. A small bilao goes fast between two.

items.name: Tamilok Kinilaw
items.where: Bakhawan Eco-Park area, Kalibo; pop-up stalls during fiestas
items.blurb: Tamilok is mangrove shipworm pulled straight from rotting bakawan logs, long and slimy and oddly oyster-like. Served kinilaw style with vinegar, ginger, onion, and chili so the texture stops freaking you out. Dare your barkada, then chew.

items.name: Chori Burger
items.where: BBQ kiosks on White Beach Station 1, near the Willy's Rock side
items.blurb: Two fat slices of chorizo griddled hard and slapped between a soft pandesal-style bun with ketchup and a sliver of cucumber. The merienda that fuels Station 1 walkers before sunset. Grab one with a Coke sa bote, sand still on your feet.

items.name: Calamansi Muffin
items.where: Real Coffee and Tea Cafe, Station 2 (original branch since 1996)
items.blurb: Real Coffee bakes a buttery muffin shot through with calamansi zest and juice, tangy on the tongue then mellow on the swallow. Pair it with their iced cafe latte for the standard Boracay breakfast loop. Locals haul boxes of six home as pasalubong.

items.name: Banana-Mango Shake
items.where: Jonah's Fruit Shake and Snack Bar, near Station 1, beachfront
items.blurb: Jonah's has been blending since 1983 and the banana-mango with milk is still the bestseller. Ripe mangoes plus lakatan bananas drink like a smoothie but taste like a fruit salad. The edible straw is fun, the cold hit after a swim is the real win.

heading: Davao City eats: tuna, durian, and smoke off the grill
intro: Davao runs on charcoal smoke, vinegar, and that famously polarizing fruit. Pack tissues for the spicy sukang Pinakurat, and napkins for the durian.

items.name: Grilled Tuna Panga
items.where: Roxas Night Market grill row along Roxas Avenue; Yellow Fin in Damosa
items.blurb: The jaw cut from General Santos tuna, brushed with toyo and calamansi then thrown over hot coals until the collar meat slides off the bone. Order it with toyomansi and a side of garlic rice, and tell the manong na panga talaga, hindi belly.

items.name: Kinilaw na Tuna
items.where: Roxas Night Market kinilaw stalls; Bankerohan Public Market eateries
items.blurb: Cubes of raw yellowfin cured in coconut vinegar with ginger, sili labuyo, calamansi, and red onion until the flesh turns opaque at the edges. Some Davao versions add tabon-tabon to cut the fishiness, so taste before you load up on extra siling labuyo.

items.name: Sinuglaw
items.where: Roxas Night Market stalls; carinderias near Matina Town Square
items.blurb: Sinugba liempo chopped into the tuna kinilaw bowl so the warm pork fat melts into the cold sour broth. The smoky-sour combo is a Mindanao invention, and Davao serves it with puso or steamed rice for the full mess-around-the-table experience.

items.name: Durian
items.where: Magsaysay Park fruit stands on R. Magsaysay Ave; Bankerohan bagsakan
items.blurb: Peak season runs August to October during Kadayawan, when Puyat and Arancillo varieties pile up at the Magsaysay stalls. Bankerohan prices are noticeably lower, but bring exact change and ask the vendor to crack open a pod so you can sniff-test before paying.

items.name: Durian Candy and Yema
items.where: Lola Abon's factory on Ecoland Drive; Apo ni Lola pasalubong center
items.blurb: Soft chewy squares that Lola Abon's has been hand-rolling since the 1950s, plus durian yema with butter from Apo ni Lola for the creamier lane. Buy boxes for the office, but eat one in the tricycle on the way back so you know what you brought home.

items.name: Mangosteen
items.where: Bankerohan Market fruit section; roadside stalls in Calinan and Toril
items.blurb: The purple queen of fruits hits Davao stalls from June through October, especially after Calinan and Toril farms harvest. Press the rind: if it gives, the segments inside are juicy white pillows, and you eat them with bare hands while the juice stains your shirt.

items.name: Imbao Soup
items.where: Times Beach paluto stalls; Bankerohan wet section for raw imbao
items.blurb: Mangrove clams from the Davao Gulf simmered in a ginger and sili broth, sometimes tinola-style with malunggay leaves, sometimes sour with batuan. The shells open up with a sweet briny liquor, so sip the soup straight from the bowl before you tackle the meat.

items.name: Suman sa Lihiya with Latik
items.where: Magsaysay Park morning vendors; Bankerohan Market kakanin row
items.blurb: Glutinous rice cooked with lye water until it turns chewy and amber, wrapped in banana leaf and topped with toasted coconut curds and muscovado syrup. Davao vendors sell it warm by the bundle in the morning, perfect with kapeng barako before you head to the market.

items.name: Pomelo
items.where: Magsaysay Park fruit stands; Bankerohan Market bagsakan
items.blurb: The Davao pink variety has a thick green rind hiding ruby segments that are sweeter and less bitter than the ones sold up north. Vendors will hack the rind off in seconds with a bolo, hand you a bag of segments, and throw in rock salt so you can dip the wedges as you walk.

heading: Eating Your Way Through Gensan: The Bariles Capital Plate by Plate
intro: Welcome to the Tuna Capital, kabayan. Locals call yellowfin "bariles" and they will feed you parts of it you didn't know existed, plus a few Maguindanao and B'laan classics to round out your day.

items.name: Grilled Tuna Panga (Sinugba)
items.where: Tiongson Arcade ihaw-ihaw strip, Tiongson Street, Barangay Lagao
items.blurb: The jaw of a yellowfin slabbed onto a smoky grate over coals until the skin crackles. Point at the chiller, the manong cooks it on the spot, and you eat it with toyo-mansi and a mound of rice.

items.name: Tuna Kinilaw
items.where: Ranchero Nuevo Kinilaw Trio at SM City General Santos, Lagao
items.blurb: Fresh bariles cubes drowned in suka, calamansi, red onion, ginger, and siling labuyo. Ranchero serves a trio platter so you can taste classic, coconut milk, and spicy versions side by side. Order it before any cooked dish.

items.name: Crispy Tuna Buntot
items.where: Ranchero Nuevo, SM City General Santos, Barangay Lagao
items.blurb: Tuna tail deep-fried hard until the cartilage cracks like chicharon. You attack it with your hands, dipping every crispy edge in spiced vinegar. Few cities outside Gensan even put this part of the fish on a menu.

items.name: Sinuglaw
items.where: Roadside ihaw-ihaw on Pioneer Avenue; Tiongson Arcade, Lagao
items.blurb: Grilled pork belly chopped fine, then folded into raw tuna kinilaw so the hot fat melts into the vinegar. Salty smoke meets sour brightness in the same spoonful. Order it with extra rice, you will need it.

items.name: Adobong Bagaybay at Bihod
items.where: Tiongson Arcade stalls, Tiongson Street, Barangay Lagao
items.blurb: Bagaybay is tuna sperm sac, bihod is the roe, and Gensan cooks both adobo-style or on a sizzling plate with onions and chili. The texture is creamy like taba ng talangka, not fishy at all if it is fresh.

items.name: Pastil
items.where: Pastilan halal eatery across the public market, Dadiangas
items.blurb: Steamed rice topped with dry shredded chicken or beef, then wrapped tight in banana leaf. This Maguindanao baon is the cheapest, fastest breakfast in town, perfect to grab before catching a van to Sarangani.

items.name: Tinagtag
items.where: Muslim vendor stalls inside KCC Mall snack section; Bula public market
items.blurb: A Maguindanao snack of ground rice and sugar fried into a lacy folded crisp that looks like a thin taco. It is crunchy, lightly sweet, and travels well in your backpack. Locals pair it with hot tablea or sweet sikwate.

items.name: Sinina Kambing
items.where: Halal carinderias around Barangay Bula and Tambilawan on Laurel Avenue
items.blurb: Goat slow-stewed in coconut milk with ginger, turmeric, and chili until the meat falls off the bone. This is the Mindanao Muslim answer to rendang, served on fiesta days. Eat it scooped over kanon manuk, the turmeric-stained rice.

items.name: Buko Halo-Halo
items.where: Tambilawan Kamayan Restaurant, Laurel Avenue, Dadiangas
items.blurb: Their signature dessert served inside a young coconut shell, with the soft buko meat scraped into shaved ice. Mongo, ube, leche flan, and sweet beans go on top, halal-style. The cool-down you want after grilled tuna and goat.

heading: What to Eat in Cagayan de Oro: A Kagay-anon Food Crawl
intro: CDO eats like the river it sits on, equal parts coconut milk, fresh catch, and corn from the Bukidnon highlands. Skip the mall food courts and let your suking tindera at Cogon and Divisoria do the driving.

items.name: Sinuglaw
items.where: Cucina Higala (Rosario Crescent) and carenderias near Divisoria
items.blurb: The Kagay-anon flex: grilled pork belly (sinugba) cubed with raw tuna kinilaw, then doused in suka, ginger, and tabon-tabon, that wild Mindanao fruit that tames the fishy bite. Order it as pulutan with cold San Mig, or over hot rice if you skipped lunch.

items.name: Puto Maya at Sikwate
items.where: Nanol's Cafe inside Cogon Public Market, dawn until rice runs out
items.blurb: The traditional Kagay-anon breakfast pair: sticky rice steamed with ginger and coconut milk, dunked in thick native chocolate brewed from tablea. Bukidnon grows the rice, the manangs slice ripe mangga on the side, and you eat it standing up at the wet market.

items.name: Binaki
items.where: Street vendors along Divisoria and stalls fronting Cogon Market
items.blurb: Steamed sweet corn tamale wrapped in its own husk, made from young white corn grated by hand, then bound with coconut milk and a touch of sugar. Buy by the dozen still warm, peel back the husk, and eat one while you walk the night market.

items.name: Humba ni Aling Violy
items.where: Humbaan ni Aling Violy, open-air stall inside Cogon Market
items.blurb: Slow-braised pork belly in soy, vinegar, black beans, and brown sugar until the fat goes jelly soft. Locals queue with their own rice and get a ladle straight from the kawa, no plating, no fuss, just sauce-soaked bao that wrecks your diet.

items.name: Pastel de Camiguin
items.where: Vjandep Bakeshop, Velez and Corrales branches
items.blurb: Pillowy soft buns stuffed with runny yema custard, the OG pasalubong from neighboring Camiguin sold mostly out of CDO. Get the classic yema box for titas back home, then sneak the ube or durian flavor for the bus ride.

items.name: Surol
items.where: Cucina Higala and heritage carenderias near Macasandig
items.blurb: Camiguin-rooted chicken ginataan brightened with kalabo, the local Mexican oregano that tastes like mint crashed into basil. Different from tinola because the broth is creamy coconut, not water, and the labuyo gives a slow burn that lingers.

items.name: Turones de Mani
items.where: La Salud and Merlas pasalubong stalls along Capistrano Street
items.blurb: Honey-glazed crushed peanuts rolled inside a thin pinipig wafer, an heirloom recipe from the 1920s that survived because every Kagay-anon lola bought a bundle for visitors. Crunchy, brittle, and a guaranteed pasalubong from a CDO trip.

items.name: Grilled Tuna Panga
items.where: Night Cafe stalls along Plaza Divisoria, Friday and Saturday nights
items.blurb: Fat tuna jaws marinated in soy, calamansi, and chili, then slapped on charcoal until the skin blisters and the cheek meat goes buttery. Pull up a monoblock, order a side of atchara and grilled isaw, and listen to the live band murder a Bisrock song.

items.name: Sylvanas
items.where: Slers and Lipton bakeshops around Hayes Street and Limketkai
items.blurb: Frozen meringue wafers sandwiching cashew buttercream, rolled in crushed cookie crumbs, kept in the chiller so it cracks on first bite. CDO and Dumaguete fight over bragging rights but the Slers version is what most Kagay-anons grew up unwrapping at merienda.

heading: Lutong Siquijodnon: 7 Bites to Hunt Around Isla del Fuego
intro: Siquijor isn't all sorcery and white sand. The food scene leans humble: market stalls, kiosks beside schools, Lola-run bakeries fired by driftwood. Bring small bills, ride slow, and follow the smell of woodsmoke.

items.name: Torta de Siquijor
items.where: Kiosk across Siquijor Integrated School, Larena market Sundays
items.blurb: An egg-heavy chiffon-style pastry leavened with tuba and scented faintly with anise. Locals say it actually tastes better a day or two old, so wrap a few in your bag for the ferry ride back to Dumaguete.

items.name: Pan Bisaya (Tinalay)
items.where: Lilibeth's Pan Bisaya, Enrique Villanueva town proper
items.blurb: Soft native bread baked in an outdoor mud oven fed with coconut husks and driftwood, no electric oven in sight. Ask for the version stuffed with sweet coconut, and time your visit before noon since batches sell out fast.

items.name: Budbud Kabog
items.where: Siquijor Public Market karinderias, Sunday stalls in Larena
items.blurb: Steamed millet logs wrapped in wilted banana leaves, dense and nubby instead of the usual sticky-rice softness. Eat it the proper way by dunking pieces straight into a tin cup of sikwate while it's still warm from the steamer.

items.name: Binatirol (Sikwate)
items.where: Small karinderias inside Siquijor Public Market, capital side
items.blurb: Frothy native hot chocolate whipped with a wooden batirol from tablea grown on the island itself. Order a mug at sunrise when the lola behind the counter is still hand-grating the cacao, and you'll see why Siquijodnons drink it daily.

items.name: Binas-oy
items.where: Fiesta tables in Lazi and San Juan, Larena market Sundays
items.blurb: A pork innards stew that reads like a leaner cousin of dinuguan, simmered with liver, intestines, and a splash of vinegar instead of being thickened with blood. Mostly a fiesta dish, so ask around for when the next barangay celebration is happening.

items.name: Kinilaw na Lapu-Lapu
items.where: Public Market wet section, Paliton and Maite grill huts in San Juan
items.blurb: Cubes of grouper bathed in coconut vinegar with ginger, sili labuyo, red onion, and a pinch of salt from local salt-makers. Buy the fish straight off the morning catch at the market and have any sutukil-style hut nearby kilaw it for you on the spot.

items.name: Saang sa Alat
items.where: Reef-edge stalls at Siquijor Public Market, Lazi and Maria coasts
items.blurb: Spider conch boiled in seawater until the meat slides out with a toothpick, served with a small saucer of suka and crushed siling labuyo. Some old-timers eat it raw, but first-timers should stick to the boiled version and slurp the briny juice from the shell.

heading: What to Eat in El Nido: Eight Plates Worth Skipping a Boat Tour For
intro: Forget the resort buffet for a second. El Nido eats hardest after the bangkas come in, when grills fire up along Corong-Corong and the market stalls start chopping. Here is the short list before your flip-flops give up.

items.name: Grilled Lapu-Lapu (Inihaw na Isda)
items.where: Corong-Corong beachfront grill row, from 5pm onward
items.blurb: Vendors line charcoal drums along the Corong-Corong sand strip and grill whatever the bangkeros pulled out that morning. Pick your fish from the bilao, wait for the char, dunk in soy-calamansi-siling labuyo.

items.name: Tamilok Kinilaw
items.where: Sibaltan side trips and stalls near El Nido Public Market
items.blurb: Locals pry these mangrove woodworms straight from bakawan logs around Sibaltan on the east coast, then soak them in coconut vinegar, ginger, and sili. Texture is slippery like a raw oyster, flavor is briny mangrove. Down it in one slurp.

items.name: Crocodile Sisig
items.where: Boodle Fight Resto and Bar on El Nido-Pancol Road
items.blurb: Palawan farms the saltwater croc, so kitchens here chop the white meat, sizzle it on a cast-iron plate with onion, calamansi, and chopped sili. Tastes closer to lean chicken thigh than anything reptilian. Order one plate for the table.

items.name: Chao Long
items.where: Stalls near El Nido Public Market terminal and Hama Street
items.blurb: Vietnamese boat people who passed through Palawan in the late 70s left this beef noodle soup behind, and El Nido picked it up. Broth runs darker and sweeter than Hanoi pho, with toasted French bread on the side for dunking.

items.name: Tuna Kinilaw sa Gata
items.where: Maa's Grill on Calle Hama and night stalls on Caalan Beach Road
items.blurb: Yellowfin tuna gets cubed raw and cured fast in coconut vinegar, calamansi, ginger, red onion, and a splash of fresh coconut cream to mellow the bite. Sili labuyo on top if you want it hot. Eat within ten minutes of plating.

items.name: Island Hopping Boodle Fight
items.where: Tour A, B, C, D banca lunches on 7 Commando or Helicopter Island
items.blurb: Your bangkero unrolls a banana leaf on the sand and dumps grilled pusit, pork barbecue, kalamansi-marinated chicken, eggplant, sliced mango, and rice across the middle. Everyone eats bare-handed while the boat anchors offshore.

items.name: Dangsilog (Danggit, Sinangag, Itlog)
items.where: Karenderyas inside El Nido Public Market and Rizal Street huts
items.blurb: Rabbitfish split, salted, sun-dried on bilaos, then fried until the edges curl. Comes with garlic fried rice, runny egg, and a sawsawan of suka with crushed garlic. The fish-rice-vinegar combo fuels every 6am island-hopping briefing.

items.name: Lato Salad
items.where: Wet stall side of El Nido Public Market on Real Street
items.blurb: Green caviar seaweed harvested from shallows around Cuyo and Linapacan, served raw with diced tomato, red onion, and a splash of bagoong or coconut vinegar. The little beads pop between your teeth and taste like clean ocean.

heading: What to Eat in Puerto Princesa: A DIY Foodie Run
intro: Puerto Princesa is where Vietnamese refugee cooking met Palawan mangroves and somehow nobody complained. Bring an empty stomach, a willingness to chew slimy things, and walking shoes for Rizal Avenue.

items.name: Tamilok
items.where: Kinabuchs Grill and Bar on Rizal Avenue
items.blurb: Mangrove woodworm pulled fresh from rotting bakauan logs, served kinilaw-style swimming in vinegar, sliced onion, and siling labuyo. Slurp it whole instead of chewing, chase with a cold San Mig, and you have bragging rights every Palaweno expects.

items.name: Crocodile Sisig
items.where: Kinabuchs Grill and Bar, Rizal Avenue
items.blurb: Farmed meat from the rescue center near Irawan gets chopped fine and thrown on a sizzling plate with calamansi and chili. Texture lands somewhere between dalag and chicken thigh, with none of the gaminess you might brace for.

items.name: Chao Long
items.where: Bona's Chaolong Haus on Manalo Street
items.blurb: Vietnamese beef noodle soup brought over by Boat People in the late 70s and adopted as the city's morning ritual. Dexter Bona bought the recipe from Ms Lanh in 2004 and still mixes the spice blend himself, same as the old refugee kitchen.

items.name: Danggit Lamayo
items.where: Breakfast stalls at San Jose New Market
items.blurb: Palawan rabbitfish split butterfly-style, soaked in vinegar with crushed bawang and paminta, then sun-dried only halfway before frying. The Puerto Princesa version comes out fatter and softer than the Cebu kind, shreds easily over sinangag.

items.name: Lato Salad
items.where: Wet section, San Jose New Market and Kalui on Rizal Avenue
items.blurb: Sea grapes harvested from Honda Bay that pop like tiny bubble wrap between your teeth. Locals dress them simply with sukang Iloko, raw red onion, and a pinch of asin, no cooking involved. Pair with grilled lapu-lapu.

items.name: Kinilaw na Tanigue
items.where: Kalui Restaurant on Rizal Avenue (reservations only)
items.blurb: Spanish mackerel cured in coconut vinegar with ginger, sili, and a splash of gata to round off the acid. Kalui plates it on bamboo with green mango ribbons and toasted coconut shavings. Show up barefoot, that is house rules.

items.name: Baker's Hill Hopia
items.where: Baker's Hill, Santa Monica Road off the National Highway
items.blurb: Flaky stacked pastry baked fresh on-site, with ube halaya, monggo, and pandan-cheese fillings as the bestsellers. Grab a box hot from the oven, eat one in the garden among the dwarf statues, then pack the rest as pasalubong.

items.name: Chicken Inasal
items.where: Ka Inato on Rizal Avenue Extension
items.blurb: Bacolod-style grilled chicken that made its way west and stuck, basted in annatto oil, calamansi, and tanglad over coconut husk coals. Ka Inato lets you spoon the chicken oil onto your rice. Ask for atchara and a toyomansi sawsawan.

heading: What to Eat in Guimaras: 8 Bites Worth the Pumpboat Ride
intro: Guimaras is small enough to circle on a tricycle in one day, but its food list runs longer than the sweetest mango season. Come with napkins and an appetite for everything that involves a carabao mango and a cashew nut.

items.name: Mango Pizza
items.where: The Pitstop Restaurant, Brgy. San Miguel, Jordan
items.blurb: A thin crust loaded with melted cheese, ripe Guimaras mango cubes, chopped cashew, and bell pepper slivers. Pitstop claims to be the only place serving it, and the sweet-salty bite makes sense the moment the first slice cools on your plate.

items.name: Mango Bulalo
items.where: The Pitstop Restaurant, San Miguel, Jordan
items.blurb: Beef shank simmered until the marrow slides out, finished with chunks of ripe carabao mango that turn the broth gently sweet. Order it for lunch after the Trappist Monastery loop so the bone marrow lands on your rice while it is still steaming.

items.name: Talaba sa Suka
items.where: Roadside oyster stalls along the Jordan-Buenavista coastal road
items.blurb: Oysters pulled straight from the Guimaras Strait, served steamed in their shells with a small bowl of spiced vinegar, siling labuyo, and crushed ginger. Locals pile them on a banana leaf and slurp them down with cold soda while waiting for the next pumpboat.

items.name: Mango Piaya
items.where: Trappist Monastic Products Gift Shop, San Miguel, Jordan
items.blurb: A flat, flaky pastry stuffed with muscovado sugar and Guimaras mango puree, baked by the Trappist monks who have been at it since the seventies. Buy a stack on the way out of the monastery because they vanish before the ferry back to Iloilo.

items.name: Mango Bars
items.where: Trappist Monastery shop, Jordan
items.blurb: A butterscotch-style square packed with dried mango chips and toasted cashew on top, cut thick enough to share but rarely shared. The monastery version is the original recipe and the box barely survives the trip home.

items.name: Kansi
items.where: Carinderias near Jordan Wharf, San Miguel poblacion
items.blurb: Beef shank boiled long with batuan fruit until the broth turns cloudy and tart, then ladled over rice with siling labuyo on the side. The local version leans heavier on souring fruit than the Iloilo one.

items.name: Ibos with Ripe Mango
items.where: Morning vendors at Jordan Wharf and Buenavista public market
items.blurb: Sticky rice cooked in coconut milk, wrapped in young coconut leaves shaped like little pyramids, and sold by the bundle before sunrise. Peel one open, dip it into a sliced ripe mango, and you have the Guimaras breakfast that fueled generations of farmers.

items.name: Mango Sorbetes
items.where: Dirty ice cream carts at Alubihod Beach and Jordan Wharf
items.blurb: Carabao mango churned into bright orange ice cream and scooped into a pandesal or a small cone right at the cart. Get yours after a swim at Alubihod so the salt on your skin and the cold sweet scoop balance out under the coconut trees.

heading: What to Eat in Lucban, Quezon: A DIY Food Crawl
intro: Lucban sits at the foot of Mount Banahaw and eats like a town that knows what it likes: garlicky sausages, slurpable noodles on banana leaf, and rice wafers bright enough to hang on a wall. Pack tissue, bring an appetite, and skip the cutlery for at least one meal.

items.name: Pancit Habhab
items.where: Buddy's on Quezon Ave, stalls fronting Lucban Public Market
items.blurb: Miki noodles tossed in pork-bone broth with cabbage, kalabasa, and bits of pork, then slapped onto a strip of banana leaf. You eat it utensil-free by tipping the leaf to your mouth and chasing each bite with sukang Quezon. The name literally describes the eating style.

items.name: Lucban Longganisa
items.where: G.B. Daza near the market, Dealo Koffee Klatch on Quezon Ave
items.blurb: Stubby red sausage colored by paminton, packed with raw garlic and oregano instead of bay leaf, with a noticeable salt-vinegar tang. Locals fry it crisp and serve with sinangag and a small dish of native vinegar. Vacuum-packed strings make solid pasalubong.

items.name: Hardinera
items.where: Buddy's Lucban and home cooks at the market during Pahiyas
items.blurb: A meatloaf in a llanera, layered with hard-boiled egg slices, bell pepper rings, and raisins over ground pork in tomato sauce. It is steamed rather than baked, so the top stays jewel-like instead of browning. Cold leftovers slice cleanly for the next day's baon.

items.name: Kiping
items.where: Krispy Kiping stall and house vendors along Calle Ibanez every May 15
items.blurb: Leaf-shaped wafer of glutinous rice paste steamed on talisay or cacao leaves, dyed fuchsia, lime, and yellow. Vendors grill it over coals until it puffs and blisters, then dust it with sugar or hand you vinegar on the side.

items.name: Broas
items.where: Pavino's Bakery and Campville's Food Industries in poblacion Lucban
items.blurb: Long, airy ladyfingers piped from egg-heavy batter and twice-baked until they shatter on contact. Pavino's keeps the old wood-oven recipe and sells them in long cellophane sleeves stamped with the family name. Dunk them in barako coffee for breakfast.

items.name: Espasol
items.where: Pavino's Bakery and pasalubong stalls fronting Lucban Church plaza
items.blurb: Soft, chalky logs of toasted rice flour cooked in coconut milk, rolled in more powdered rice so they squeak when you bite. Lucban's version skews drier than Laguna's, with a clean coconut finish rather than syrupy sweetness. Sold by the half-dozen wrapped in waxed paper.

items.name: Pilipit
items.where: Pasalubong rows at Lucban market and vendors heading to Tayabas
items.blurb: Twisted sticky rice dough cooked with kalabasa, then fried and glazed in caramelized brown sugar like banana cue. Far chewier than the brittle Manila pilipit, it stays pliant for hours and snaps in two satisfying coils. Buy them warm if you can.

items.name: Sinukmani
items.where: Banana-leaf trays at Lucban Public Market and Hometown Goodies
items.blurb: Local name for biko, made with malagkit cooked low in coconut milk and muscovado, then crowned with thick latik curds toasted to a deep brown. Lucban kitchens cut it into diamonds in clay bilao instead of llanera. Best eaten warm with very hot kapeng barako.

items.name: Pako Salad
items.where: Kamayan sa Palaisdaan on the Tayabas-Lucban road
items.blurb: Fiddlehead ferns from streambanks around Banahaw, blanched and tossed with itlog na maalat, kamatis, and red onion. A sweet-tart dressing ties it together, with a whole sardine on top for the bold. Eat with your hands inside a floating bamboo hut.

heading: What to Eat in Lucena City, Quezon: A Local-Style Food Crawl
intro: Lucena is the Queen City of the South, and her royal court runs on noodles, garlicky sausage, and yema-glazed cake. Skip the chain joints near the bus terminal and follow the lolas and jeepney drivers to where the real Quezonin grub lives.

items.name: Pancit Chami
items.where: Hacienda Inn on Quezon Ave Ext; Plaza Food House fronting Cathedral
items.blurb: Lucena's home-grown noodle plate of fat miki tossed in sweet-savory sauce with squid balls, pork chunks, and kikiam. Hacienda has been frying it on the same griddle since the 80s, while Plaza sits across the San Ferdinand church.

items.name: Pancit Habhab
items.where: Buddy's on Quezon Ave; carinderias along Merchan Street
items.blurb: A Lucban native that migrated down the road, served on banana leaf so you can shovel it in without forks. The miki are dried then stir-fried with kabute, pechay, and pork, then drowned in spiced sukang Quezon.

items.name: Lucban Longganisa
items.where: Lucena Public Market dry-goods row; Pasalubong Center Diversion Rd
items.blurb: Garlicky, oregano-heavy, stained orange from paprika, this sausage is shorter and chunkier than the sweet Pampanga kind. Locals fry it crisp, eat it with sinangag and atchara, and dunk every bite in vinegar with siling labuyo.

items.name: Hardinera
items.where: Buddy's Lucena; fiesta eateries near San Fernando Cathedral
items.blurb: Lucban-born meatloaf baked inside a leche flan llanera, packed with ground pork, hotdog rounds, raisins, pineapple, boiled egg, and liver spread. Comes out like an upside-down terrine tasting of menudo and embutido.

items.name: Crispy Pata
items.where: Bubbles Crispy Pata & Restaurant on Quezon Ave
items.blurb: Bubbles has been the worst-kept secret in town for deep-fried pork knuckle with skin that shatters and meat that pulls off the bone. Order the soy-vinegar-siling-haba dip and ask for extra rice because one pata feeds three backpackers.

items.name: Yema Cake
items.where: Rodillas Yema Cake near Chowking on Diversion Road
items.blurb: Tayabas-style chiffon smothered in thick yema custard made from condensed milk and egg yolks, no buttercream involved. Rodillas is the originator and packs it in pink boxes that survive the 4-hour bus ride back to Cubao.

items.name: Broas
items.where: Dealo Koffee Klatch on Quezon Ave; Grand Terminal stalls
items.blurb: Crumbly egg-and-vanilla ladyfingers from neighboring Sariaya that taste like a sturdier, browner mamon. Dealo bakes them daily and sells the broken bits cheaper, perfect for dunking in kapeng barako while waiting for your jeep to fill.

items.name: Puto Bao
items.where: Lucena Public Market kakanin row; sidewalk vendors at Grand Terminal
items.blurb: An Agdangan and Unisan specialty with a violet ube-tinted rice cake shell and a sticky filling of latik and caramelized coconut. Eat it warm off the bilao before the coconut hardens, paired with native tsokolate.

items.name: Taipao
items.where: Hacienda Inn on Quezon Ave Ext; SM Lucena food court
items.blurb: Lucena's oversized siopao stuffed with asado pork, Chinese sausage, salted egg, and a whole boiled egg, big enough that one fills a backpacker for a full bus ride. Hacienda steams them to order so the dough stays pillowy.

heading: Antipolo Eats: Suman, Kasoy, and Snacks From the Pilgrim Trail
intro: Antipolo runs on sticky rice, roasted nuts, and the post-Mass munchies of pilgrims coming down from the Cathedral. Here is what to actually put in your mouth between the Hinulugang Taktak hike and the jeepney ride home.

items.name: Suman sa Ibos
items.where: Pasalubong Center stalls beside Antipolo Cathedral, P. Burgos Street
items.blurb: Unsweetened malagkit rice cooked in gata and bundled inside young coconut palm leaves called kampil. Peel the wrap and dunk the warm log into latik or white sugar. Aling Carmen and Aling Mareng are the names devotees keep returning to.

items.name: Kasoy (Roasted Cashew)
items.where: Cashew vendors around the Cathedral and Sumulong Highway
items.blurb: Antipolo sits on cashew tree country, so the kasoy here is bigger and sweeter than supermarket packs. Buy it plain salted for the road, or grab the garlic version that Sumulong vendors scoop straight from the tin.

items.name: Kalamay sa Latik
items.where: Aling Carmen and kakanin stalls inside Pasalubong Center
items.blurb: Ground glutinous rice simmered with coconut milk and brown sugar until it turns into a glossy, chewy slab. Finished with a dark coconut glaze and toasted latik curds on top. Comes in banana leaf, perfect for slicing at a Taktak picnic.

items.name: Suman sa Ibos with Langka
items.where: Aling Mareng stall, Pasalubong Center beside Antipolo Cathedral
items.blurb: A langka-laced spin on classic ibos, with ripe jackfruit strips folded into the malagkit before steaming. The fruit perfume seeps into the coconut leaf wrap so you smell it before you bite. Pair with hot kapeng barako.

items.name: Mangga and Bagoong
items.where: Fruit tables in front of the Cathedral and along Taktak Road
items.blurb: The third leg of the SuMaKaH trio, served unripe and crunchy with a small sachet of fermented shrimp paste. Vendors slice it Hassel style on request so you can walk and dip while you queue for the Virgen ng Antipolo.

items.name: Puto Bumbong
items.where: Bamboo tube stalls outside Antipolo Cathedral during Simbang Gabi
items.blurb: Purple pirurutong rice steamed inside bamboo tubes, then rolled in margarine, muscovado, and grated coconut. The Cathedral grounds turn into a puto bumbong row from December 16 onward, with vendors fanning the steamer coals all night.

items.name: Bibingka
items.where: Clay pot stalls along Circumferential Road and the Cathedral plaza
items.blurb: Rice cake baked in a clay palayok lined with banana leaf, fired top and bottom with live coconut husk charcoal. The Antipolo version comes thick, with itlog na maalat and a slab of kesong puti melting into the crust.

items.name: Adobong Kasoy
items.where: Pidiong’s and kasoy specialty stalls inside Pasalubong Center
items.blurb: Toasted Antipolo cashews tossed in soy, vinegar, and a heavy hand of garlic until every nut wears a sticky brown coat. The snack version of adobo, sold by the 250 gram pack so you can ration it on the trike home.

items.name: Coco Jam (Latik na Matamis)
items.where: Aling Carmen and small batch jam sellers, Pasalubong Center
items.blurb: Slow cooked coconut milk and muscovado reduced into a dark brown spread sold in recycled glass jars. Families ladle it over hot suman or smear it on pandesal at merienda. Ask when the batch was cooked, fresher jars taste smokier.

heading: Eat Your Way Through Bohol: Tagbilaran and Loboc Field Notes
intro: Bohol is more than Chocolate Hills and tarsiers, pare. The province has its own pantry of sticky sweets, river catch, and Hokkien-rooted braises that you will only really understand if you eat where the lolas are lining up.

items.name: Puto Maya at Sikwate
items.where: Cogon Public Market stalls, Tagbilaran (5 to 7 AM)
items.blurb: Coconut-cooked sticky rice steamed in banana leaf, dunked into thick sikwate from local tablea, with a side of ripe carabao mango. The stalls around Cogon have been doing this since the 1970s, and the trick is to eat all three together, not separately.

items.name: Peanut Kisses
items.where: Bohol Quality (BQ) Mall outlet, CPG Avenue, Tagbilaran
items.blurb: Peaked little meringues studded with crushed Garcia-Hernandez peanuts, shaped to mimic the Chocolate Hills. Buy them straight from the BQ outlet on Carlos P. Garcia Avenue so you get the soft-centered fresh batch, not the dry mall-shelf version.

items.name: Calamay sa Jagna
items.where: Jagna town stalls and Cogon Market pasalubong row, Tagbilaran
items.blurb: Glutinous rice slow-cooked with coconut milk and muscovado for four hours until it stretches like taffy, then sealed inside a polished coconut shell with red crepe paper. The JACAMCO cooperative still gatekeeps the proper Jagna version, so check the seal before you buy.

items.name: Broas (Baclayon Ladyfingers)
items.where: Osang's and pastry row behind Baclayon Church, 8 km from Tagbilaran
items.blurb: Crisp piped sponge fingers made from just egg, sugar, and flour, hand-piped onto tin sheets the way Rosa Clarin started doing in the 1800s. Baclayon is literally called the Broas Capital, and Osang's bakery at the back of the old stone church is the original.

items.name: Ubi Kinampay
items.where: Dauis Public Market and Aproniana Gift Shop, Tagbilaran
items.blurb: The deep-violet, vanilla-perfumed yam variety locals call the queen of Philippine ubi, namechecked in the Boholano hymn itself. Eat it as halaya jam, in pastillas, or folded into ice cream, but make sure the label says kinampay specifically, not generic ube.

items.name: Humba Bisaya
items.where: Payag Restaurant and Gerarda's Family Restaurant, Tagbilaran
items.blurb: Pork belly slow-braised with soy, vinegar, brown sugar, tausi, star anise, and dried banana blossoms, a Hokkien hong-bah that Boholanos made sweeter than its Luzon adobo cousin. Order it with plain rice, never garlic rice, so you taste the syrupy gravy properly.

items.name: Loboc River Crayfish (Kasag sa Suba)
items.where: Riverside stalls and Venia's Kitchen, Loboc town proper
items.blurb: Freshwater crayfish hand-caught from the Loboc River, usually sauteed in garlic butter or stewed in coconut milk with malunggay. They are seasonal and small-batch, so ask the floating restaurant captain in the morning if any came in that day.

items.name: Humbang Langka
items.where: Amore Cruises Village Floating Restaurant, Loboc River
items.blurb: Young jackfruit stewed humba-style in soy, garlic, and lemongrass until it shreds like pork, the signature plate of the Loboc floating buffet circuit. It is the one dish on the bamboo-raft cruise that you will not get on the Tagbilaran side.

items.name: Kinilaw na Malasugue
items.where: Tagbilaran wet market stalls and Garden Cafe, Loboc
items.blurb: Raw blue marlin or tanigue cured in tuba vinegar and calamansi, sharpened with ginger, red onion, siling labuyo, and a splash of coconut milk to round the bite. The Boholano version leans heavier on the gata than the Cebuano kinilaw, which is how you tell them apart.

heading: What to Eat in San Juan, La Union Between Surf Sessions
intro: Elyu food hits different after a morning paddle. You are sitting in Ilocandia, so expect a lot of pork, a lot of vinegar, and a lot of macapuno sneaking into your dessert spoon.

items.name: Bagnet
items.where: Dalikan Bagnet Atbp, MacArthur Highway strip, Urbiztondo
items.blurb: Deep-fried pork belly twice-cooked until the rind shatters when you press it with a spoon. Eat it the Ilocano way with sukang Iloko, sliced tomato, and bagoong on the side so the acid cuts the fat.

items.name: Pinakbet
items.where: Makan at Eliseos, 379 Saint Jude Subdivision, San Juan
items.blurb: The original Ilocano version, not the Tagalog one swimming in liquid. Squash, ampalaya, talong, sitaw, and winged beans get tossed in a kawali with fish bagoong and crowned with crumbled bagnet.

items.name: Poqui-poqui
items.where: Makan at Eliseos and carinderia row near San Juan public market
items.blurb: Charred eggplant mashed with sauteed tomato, onion, garlic, and scrambled egg until it turns into a smoky savory spread. Pinoy travelers love it for breakfast with longganisa because the smokiness wakes you up.

items.name: Dinakdakan
items.where: Makan at Eliseos and roadside ihawan past Sebay Surf Central
items.blurb: Boiled then grilled pig ear and cheek, chopped fine and bound with pig brain or mayo, then brightened with calamansi, ginger, and siling labuyo. The crunchy cartilage plus creamy sauce combo is the whole point.

items.name: Vigan-style empanada
items.where: Gem's Empanada at Robinsons Place La Union
items.blurb: Orange achuete dough stuffed with grated green papaya, togue, a whole egg, and a coil of garlicky Vigan longganisa, deep-fried to order. You bite a hole, drizzle sukang Iloko inside, and slurp before the yolk runs.

items.name: Ilocano miki
items.where: Carinderia stalls along Quezon Avenue and the Poblacion public market
items.blurb: Thick yellow egg noodles in pork broth tinted orange from achuete, topped with sliced boiled egg and crumbled bagnet. Splash in sukang Iloko at the table and you will taste why this dish travelled down from Vigan.

items.name: Tuna panga
items.where: Kabsat in Urbiztondo and Bay-Bay Seafood and Grill, Ciabel Hotel
items.blurb: Grilled tuna jaw the size of your forearm, the meat between the bones is the buttery part everyone fights over. Squeeze calamansi, dip in toyomansi with siling labuyo, and pick it apart with your fingers.

items.name: Halo-Halo de Iloko
items.where: Halo-Halo de Iloko Balay, nearby San Fernando
items.blurb: Each ingredient comes from a different La Union town, so you get Agoo latik, Bacnotan ube, Balaoan sticky rice, and San Juan macapuno in one bowl. They crumble polvoron on top instead of pinipig, which surprisingly works.

items.name: Basi
items.where: Sari-sari stores in Naguilian and Bacnotan, San Juan pasalubong stalls
items.blurb: Fermented sugarcane wine aged in burnay jars with samak bark for a tannic edge, around 14 percent alcohol on a good batch. Locals will tell you the Casa Blanca label from Bacnotan is the safe bet for a souvenir bottle.
